rulings.Case Title: JOHN GIDDING et al VS. IVAN KAYE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Order To Show Cause In Re Contempt (John Giddings)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, December 6, 2013, Line 1, Order To Show Cause In Re Contempt (John Giddings). Pro Tem Judge Geoff Howard, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Judgment creditor Glendonbrook Wines moved for civil contempt after judgment debtor John Gidding failed to respond to post-judgment discovery relating to enforcement of the judgment obtained by Glendonbrook against Gidding. On August 23, 2013, this Court granted Glendonbrook's motion to compel responses to the post-judgment discovery. On October 10, 2013, the Court continued the hearing date to allow Glendonbrook to personally serve Gidding. On October 31, 2013, this Court issued an Order to Show Cause in Re Contempt, in which the Court indicated that Gidding's appeared to be contempt of the Court's October 31, 2013 Order, and ordering Gidding to appear on December 6, 2013 to show cause as to why he should not be held in contempt. In connection with its original motion to hold judgment debtor in contempt, Glendonbrook served Gidding at the address provided by him to the Court of Appeals, and also by email to the email address used by Gidding to object to Glendonbrook's notice of levy against a bank account. However, Gidding now appears to moved permanently to Luxembourg. Glendonbrook has provided evidence that it would be costly and time-consuming to effect personal service in Luxembourg, in part because all papers must be translated into a foreign language even though Gidding is a US citizen who speaks fluent English. In addition, Gidding has obviously received notice of the contempt motion because he has referenced it in papers filed objecting to the notice of levy. However, Glendonbrook requests the Court continue the date for the show cause hearing for 120 days to allow Glendonbrook to attempt personal service. The Court grants this request and resets the order to show cause hearing for April 11, 2014 at 9:00 a.m. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to geoff.howard@bingham.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: BENTLY RESERVE, L.P., A CALIFORIA LIMITED et al VS. ANDREAS G. PAPALIOLIOS, AN INDIVIDUAL, AKA "SAL R" et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, December 6, 2013, Line 7: CROSS DEFENDANTS BENTLY RESERVE, L.P.'S, a California limited partnership, and BENTLY NOB HILL, L.P.'S, a California limited partnership, DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT The requests for judicial notice are granted. Cross-Defendant Christopher Bently's ("Mr. Bently") demurrer is sustained with 20 days leave to amend for failure to state a cause of action. (CCP § 430.10(e).) The facts pled in the Cross-Complaint do not sufficiently plead a breach of the Settlement Agreement. The purported breach of the stay-away agreement is barred by the statute of limitations. The Cross-Complaint does not relate back to the date of filing of the original complaint because the allegations do not arise from the same transaction, occurrence, or series of transactions or occurrences. Additionally, the Cross-Complaint does not sufficiently plead that Mr. Bently violated any stay-away order; for example, it does not plead that Mr. Bently was ever within 50 yards of Cross-Complainant Andreas Papaliolios ("Mr. Papaliolios"). As to the purported failure to withdraw reports made to credit agencies, the Settlement Agreement did not obligate Mr. Bently to do so, and therefore, there is no alleged breach based on the facts pled. As to the alleged disparagement, the Cross-Complaint does not establish that Mr. Bently engaged in any of the acts pleaded. The Cross-Complaint alleges that Mr. Papaliolios was asked to leave the Bently Reserve Building by an employee of Bently Reserve. Bently Reserve was not a party to the settlement agreement, and there appear to be no alter ego or agency allegations in the Cross-Complaint. Additionally, as pled, the act of a property owner asking a person to leave private property does not amount to the plain-language definition of disparagement. The right to exclude others is "one of the most essential sticks in the bundle of rights that are commonly characterized by property." (Dolan v. City of Tigard ((1994) 512 U.S. 374, 384.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/MJM)

rulings.Case Title: CHARLES HARB et al VS. DEREK SENE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn Compelling The Custodian/Pmq Of State Farm General Ins. Co. To Produce Documents At Deposition (S)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, December 6, 2013, Line 2, PLAINTIFFs CHARLES HARB and THE CHARLES HARB REVOCABLE LIVING TRUST's Motion Compelling The Custodian/PMQ Of State Farm General Ins. Co. To Produce Documents At Deposition(s). Pro Tem Judge Geoff Howard, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's motion to compel insurance policies is denied. This matter involves an allegations of negligence against an insurance agent related to coverage of destruction of a house, which was insured through a homeowner's policy. Plaintiff moves to compel other insurance policies issued by State Farm to plaintiff unrelated to the property at issue in the litigation. Plaintiff asserts the other policies relate to the "relationship" between the allegedly negligent agent and State Farm. However, State Farm has conceded that the agent was acting as State Farm's agent and that State Farm is responsible for any verdict against the agent. Accordingly, the other policies are not relevant and not calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to geoff.howard@bingham.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: GRANT S. LYDDON VS. CHARLES J. WISCH et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion 1) To Compel Further Responses To Form Interrogatories; And 2) For Sanctions;
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, December 6, 2013, Line 3, CROSS COMPLAINANTs CHARLES WISCH and BARBARA FARLEY's Motion 1) To Compel Further Responses To Form Interrogatories; And 2) For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Geoff Howard, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is denied without prejudice. The interrogatory responses are generally deficient because they do not appear to state facts within responding party's possession, custody and control. For example, it would appear that Lyddon can identify witnesses with specificity and identify certain relevant documents, even if additional relevant documents may be identified during discovery. However, the motion was premature. Counsel is expected to make a reasonable effort to meet and confer in good faith. Where there is a distinct pattern of responding to meet and confer requests as demonstrated here, and moving party's counsel is on notice of opposing counsel's unavailability, it is not reasonable to file a motion after counsel fails to respond to two letters (the second during counsel's stated period of unavailability), with no effort to contact counsel by phone or in person, and where the motion filing deadline has not passed. Counsel shall meet and confer in person in an effort to resolve the outstanding issues. Moving party may re-file its motion after the meet and confer is complete and if supplemental responses are still deficient. The Court will award sanctions to the prevailing party on any such renewed motion relating to Interrogatory 15.1. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to geoff.howard@bingham.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MARK EMMETT WILSON VS. MIGUEL ALEJANDRO RESCALVO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Compel Responses To Judicial Form Interrogatories Disc-001 & Disc-002 (Ccp 2030.300(A)(3); And Request For Monetary Sanctions (Ccp 2023.010(E) And 2023.030(A)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, December 6, 2013, Line 4, PLAINTIFF MARK WILSON's Motion To Compel Responses To Judicial Form Interrogatories Disc-001 & Disc-002 (CCP 2030.300(A)(3); And Request For Monetary Sanctions (CCP 2023.010(E) And 2023.030(A)). (Part 1 of 2). Pro Tem Judge Geoff Howard, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff has brought employment-related and defamation claims against his former employer and its parent company, Det Norske Veritas AS. On the selected record before the court, an employee of the parent company, which is the relevant defendant for this motion, appears to have been involved in a managerial capacity over plaintiff's employment. Plaintiff moves to compel responses to general form interrogatories and employment form interrogatories. Plaintiff's motion to compel is granted in part and denied in part. Det Norske Veritas AS is a party to the litigation, having been stipulated in by the parties. Plaintiff has made a sufficient showing that Defendant Det Norske Veritas AS was involved, through its employee Stein, in plaintiff's hiring, compensation and termination as to warrant discovery responses from Defendant. Although Defendant may prevail on its pending demurrer, discovery responses are not the proper vehicle to argue for dismissal from the action. Defendant's argument that a different defendant has provided responsive information is not persuasive. Plaintiff is entitled to any separate knowledge or information within the possession of each defendant, both because that information may be different, but also because it may be relevant to later motion practice and/or trial. Defendant also does not adequately respond to plaintiff's authority regarding what constitutes a proper privilege objection. Accordingly, defendant is ordered to fully respond to both sets of form interrogatories, with the exception of Employment form interrogatories 201.5-201.7, which the Court finds not relevant to this matter, within 10 days of entry of the Court's order. To the extent defendant intends to rely on a privilege objection in its supplemental responses, it must fully comply with the rules relating to such objections, including specifying the privilege on which it relies and providing sufficient information to plaintiff to allow plaintiff to evaluate the propriety of the asserted privilege. (See part 2.) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: LAW OFFICE OF EDWARD M. HIGGINBOTHAM VS. FRANK MYERS et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn To Compel Discovery Responses, Including Responses To Interrogatories, Admissions And Document Requests For Monetary And Terminating Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, December 6, 2013, Line 5, PLAINTIFF LAW OFFICE OF EDWARD M. HIGGINBOTHAM's Motion To Compel Discovery Responses, Including Responses To Interrogatories, Admissions And Document Requests For Monetary And Terminating Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Geoff Howard, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The Court previously ordered defendant to provide "complete, verified Discovery responses" and awarded sanctions for defendant's prior failure to respond to discovery or to meet and confer attempts. Plaintiff now renews its motion after receiving inadequate supplemental responses and after having provided seven extensions to the discovery response deadline. Defendant has not opposed the motion. The Court previously ruled on the relevance of the discovery responses through its prior order that defendant provide complete, verified responses, and therefore the Court does not revisit that issue here. Instead, the question before the Court is whether defendant has complied with the Court's prior order. The Court concludes that defendant has not provided "complete, verified" responses. Accordingly, plaintiff's motion to compel discovery response and for monetary sanctions in the amount of $4,590 is granted. Defendant is ordered to appear before this Court on January 23, 2014 and to show cause as to why the Court should not grant plaintiff's request for evidentiary sanctions. Plaintiff shall submit any further briefing it wishes to provide in connection with that hearing. Plaintiff shall also submit a proposed order in advance of that hearing that identifies what liability facts alleged in plaintiff's complaint are sufficiently related to the deficient discovery responses that they should be deemed conclusively established as a further discovery sanction. Plaintiff shall also submit a courtesy copy of the operative complaint. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to geoff.howard@bingham.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
